brief_document.title: Determinism linter, generated test scaffolds, and input skeletons
brief_document.purpose: The cross-cutting moves that separate good from best-in-class (RM-029). A static determinism gate turns the determinism boundary into a CI check that complements the SDK's type-level structural block — a provable-determinism guarantee no competitor offers. Alongside it, generated test skeletons and input skeletons mean testing and triggering a workflow start from a scaffold, not a blank file.
brief_document.task: Add the quality and ergonomics scaffolds that ride on the codegen. In: `aion check --deterministic`, a static lint that flags any wall-clock or entropy call reachable from workflow code; generated aion/testing skeletons per workflow (each activity mocked, a clock advance per timer, a replay-determinism assertion); and `aion input <workflow_type>`, which emits a valid input skeleton from the workflow's input type. Out: RM-003's polymorphic --input and client-side validation (separate dispatch-time work), and any new test framework (the scaffolds target the existing aion/testing harness).

requirements.title: aion check --deterministic static gate
requirements.spec: WHEN `aion check --deterministic` runs over a workflow module, THE SYSTEM SHALL flag any wall-clock or entropy call reachable from workflow code and SHALL pass a workflow that reaches none.
acceptance: A positive fixture (a workflow that reaches a wall-clock or entropy call) fails the check.
A negative fixture (a clean workflow) passes the check.
The check is usable as a CI gate, exiting non-zero on a violation (C28).

requirements.title: Generated test scaffolds against aion/testing
requirements.spec: WHEN `aion generate` runs, THE SYSTEM SHALL emit an aion/testing skeleton per workflow: each activity pre-mocked, a clock advance per timer, and a replay-determinism assertion. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT introduce a new test framework.
acceptance: The generated skeleton mocks each of the workflow's activities and advances the simulated clock once per timer.
The generated skeleton includes a replay-determinism assertion using the existing aion/testing harness (C29).
No new test framework is introduced — the skeleton targets aion/testing.

requirements.title: aion input skeleton from the workflow input type
requirements.spec: WHEN `aion input <workflow_type>` runs, THE SYSTEM SHALL emit a valid input skeleton derived from the workflow's input type.
acceptance: `aion input <workflow_type>` emits an input skeleton derived from the workflow's input type (C30).
The emitted skeleton round-trips through the workflow's input codec without a decode error.
The skeleton is generated from the type, not hand-written.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement RM-003's polymorphic --input or client-side input validation — that is separate dispatch-time work; this delivers only the type-derived skeleton.
SHALL NOT introduce a new test framework — generated scaffolds target the existing aion/testing harness.
SHALL NOT add default values to generated skeletons (ADR-001).
verification: Positive and negative determinism fixtures prove the gate both directions.
The generated test skeleton compiles and runs under gleam test; the input skeleton validates against the input codec.

adrs.title: No arbitrary limits, no assumed defaults
adrs.decision: Configurable values come from the builder/author or are deferred to the layer that owns them (e.g. beamr's own defaults). No caps, rate limits, or hardcoded defaults invented at the aion layer. Values are discussed before implementation. Rejected: convenience defaults — they are decisions made for the user without telling them.

checklist.text: `aion check --deterministic` statically flags any wall-clock or entropy call reachable from workflow code and passes a clean workflow, proven by a positive and a negative fixture.
`aion generate` emits an aion/testing skeleton per workflow: each activity pre-mocked, a clock advance per timer, and a replay-determinism assertion.
`aion input <workflow_type>` emits a valid input skeleton derived from the workflow's input type, so an input document is never hand-written from scratch.

stories.text: As a developer, I want a static check that flags any non-deterministic call reachable from workflow code, so that I can prove a workflow is replay-safe in CI rather than discovering a desync in production.
As a developer, I want a generated test skeleton and an input skeleton for each workflow, so that testing and triggering a workflow start from a valid scaffold instead of a blank file.

constraints.text: Generated code is regenerable and drift-gated: `aion generate --check` reproduces every generated file byte-identical or fails; no generated file is hand-maintained alongside the source (ADR-002 forbids parallel sources; mirrors the existing aion codegen --check).

No defaults in generated configuration (ADR-001): codegen never emits a retry policy, timeout, backoff, or any value the author did not choose; a required value stays a required hole, never a silent default.

The determinism boundary is unchanged (invariant 2): generation emits activity plumbing and handler stubs, never side-effecting bodies; generated workflow code still routes every side effect through workflow.run, and generated code introduces no wall-clock or entropy path.

Production parity: the dev loop and dev UI run the real engine, the real store, and the real event stream; there is no mock-only execution path whose semantics diverge from production, and activity mocking is opt-in per run, not a separate engine.

No second source of truth for introspection (ADR-007): time-travel, the determinism diff, and the live canvas overlay read the existing event history and deterministic replay; no parallel debug log or state store is introduced.

The visual canvas is a projection (ADR-014): it is generated from the typed source; any structural round-trip is bounded and regenerates Gleam that still type-checks; the canvas never becomes the authoritative artifact.

aion-toolchain shells out to the gleam binary and stays optional: aion and aion-server carry no compiler dependency; without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion files only (the DESIGN-OVERVIEW boundary).
Thin-workflow respected (ADR-012): generated activities thread sealed payloads plus thin facts; codegen does not reintroduce heavy decode or prompt rendering into the deterministic workflow process.

Library-code purity: clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings and cargo fmt --check pass clean; gleam format --check clean for SDK changes; no unwrap/expect/allow/expect-attr in library code; every touched file stays under 500 LOC excluding tests.

resolved_context.intention: Authoring a durable workflow on Aion should feel like writing one typed Gleam function and a file of typed activity signatures — and nothing else. Everything that is mirrored today (worker handlers, registration lists, manifest entries, codecs, JSON schemas, wire-compat goldens, test skeletons) is generated from that typed source; everything that is observed (a run's state, its determinism, its shape) is projected from the event history the engine already records. When this cluster is done, the gap between 'I have a typed workflow' and 'I have a durable, deployed, debuggable workflow' is a save and a glance, not a day of hand-mirroring four files that must agree byte-for-byte. The authoring language stays Gleam — the type system is the contract and Aion's sharpest edge — and the declarative and visual surfaces become projections of that typed source, never a second place to author from. This is the experience an author reaches for, the loop they iterate in, the lens they debug through, and the canvas a non-coding stakeholder reads, all anchored to one source of truth.
